---
title: "Project-1-Checkpoint-3"
format: html
editor: visual
---

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(httr)
library(countrycode)
library(glue)
library(jsonlite)
```

## Country Source #1

### Setup

We load our democracy data and filter to 2000 onward to avoid issues with historical countries like the Soviet Union that no longer exist and won't have GDP data.

```{r}
#| message: false
democracy_data <- read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2024/2024-11-05/democracy_data.csv')

democracy_recent <- democracy_data |>
  filter(year >= 2000)
```

### Intro

We use the API-Ninjas GDP endpoint to get historical GDP data for each country. The API returns year-by-year GDP statistics including GDP per capita and GDP growth rate. We iterate over all unique countries using map().

### API Key

```{r}

my_key <- "pv2dwTZ4RCa3KghkCHIK1P2Axb4hdYr051DxyAMp"
```

### API Function

takes a 3-letter country code and returns a GDP data frame for that country.

```{r}
get_gdp <- function(country, key = my_key) {
  
  url <- glue("https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/gdp?country={country}&X-Api-Key={key}")
  my_data <- safely(fromJSON)(url)
  
  if (is.null(my_data$result)) {
    return(NULL)
  } else {
    return(my_data$result)
  }
}

```

### Iterating all the countries from API data set

```{r}

codes <- democracy_recent |>
  distinct(country_code) |>
  pull(country_code)

gdp_list <- map(codes, get_gdp)

gdp_df <- gdp_list |>
  compact() |>
  bind_rows()

write_csv(gdp_df, "C:/Users/jackl/STAT 431/Projects/Project-1-Gov-Types/gdp_data.csv")
```

### Combining to the original data set

```{r}

combined <- democracy_recent |>
  left_join(gdp_df, by = c("country_code" = "country", "year"))

```

## Country Source #2

### Dataset #2 Description (Country Life Expectancy)

This dataset includes country-level health, economic, and demographic numbers that are associated with life expectancy. Some examples are immunization rates, mortality rates, and economic variables such as GDP and health expenditure. The data accounts for 178 countries from 2000 to 2015. This dataset was obtained from Kaggle (by Amir Hossein Mirzaei).

### Loading in and Cleaning the Countries Dataset

```{r}
# load in package that allows us to convert country names to codes
library(countrycode)

# load in countries dataset
life_expectancy <- read.csv("C:/Users/jackl/Downloads/life_expectancy.csv")

# create a new variable containing each country's iso3c code
life_expectancy$country_code <-  countrycode(life_expectancy$Country, 
            origin = "country.name", 
            destination = "iso3c", 
            custom_match = c("Netherlands Antilles" = "ANT",
                             "Virgin Islands" = "VIR"))

write.csv(life_expectancy, "C:/Users/jackl/STAT 431/Projects/Project-1-Gov-Types/life_expectancy.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```

### Combining to the original data set

```{r}
meta_data <- combined |>
  left_join(life_expectancy, by = c("country_code" = "country_code", "year" = "Year"))
```

## Final Meta Data Set

```{r}
names(meta_data)
```

## Data Summary #1

### GDP by Different Regimes

```{r}
prop <- function(variable){
  meta_data |>
    filter(.data[[variable]] == 1) |>
    summarise(
      avg_gdp = mean(gdp_per_capita_nominal, na.rm = TRUE),
      n = n()
    )
}

vars <- c("is_monarchy", "is_commonwealth", "is_presidential",
          "is_colony", "is_communist")

result <- map_dfr(vars, function(var) {
  prop(var) |>
    mutate(variable = var)
})
result
```

Our findings show that colonies have the highest average GDP among the categories listed, followed closely by monarchies, while commonwealth and presidential systems fall in the middle range. In contrast, communist countries have the lowest average GDP by a large margin. The differences in sample sizes (n) across groups suggest that some categories, such as presidential regimes, are much more widely represented while others are based on less observations which may influence the averages.

## Visualization #2

### Life Expectancy by GDP

```{r}
ggplot(
  meta_data |> drop_na(gdp_per_capita_nominal, Life.expectancy),
  aes(x = gdp_per_capita_nominal, y = Life.expectancy)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_log10() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between GDP per Capita and Life Expectancy",
    subtitle = "Countries over time",
    x = "GDP per Capita (USD)",
    y = "Life Expectancy (years)"
  )
```

The chart shows a clear positive relationship between GDP per capita and life expectancy, with higher income countries experiencing longer lifespans. At lower income levels life expectancy varies more than higher income levels, suggesting other factors also play a role, while middle and high income countries show more consistent and higher life expectancy. The trend line indicates that increases in income are linked to longer lives.
